name: Jamaica
tagline: From the Taino land of Xaymaca to a sovereign Caribbean nation whose reggae and runners carry its name across the world.
summary: Jamaica's history runs from the Arawak-speaking Taino, who called the island Xaymaca, through Columbus's arrival in 1494 and a Spanish colony that all but annihilated them. The English conquest of 1655 built a brutal sugar-and-slavery economy, the wealth and wickedness of buccaneer Port Royal, and a centuries-long struggle for freedom led by the Maroons of the Blue and John Crow Mountains and by rebels from Tacky to Sam Sharpe. Emancipation in 1838 and the Morant Bay rebellion of 1865 gave way to the Pan-Africanism of Marcus Garvey, the rise of Rastafari, a labour movement, and the two-party democracy of Bustamante and Manley. Independence came on 6 August 1962, since when this small nation has projected outsized cultural and sporting power through reggae, Bob Marley, and the world's fastest sprinters.

founding.label: Independence from Britain
founding.detail: Jamaica became an independent nation within the Commonwealth on 6 August 1962, ending more than three centuries of English and then British colonial rule that had begun with the conquest of 1655. Independence followed the collapse of the West Indies Federation, which Jamaica voted to leave in a referendum on 19 September 1961, and a general election won that April by Alexander Bustamante, who became the country's first prime minister. The new state retained the British monarch as head of state, represented by a governor-general, with Norman Manley's People's National Party and Bustamante's Jamaica Labour Party as the two pillars of its democracy.

quickFacts.label: Indigenous name
quickFacts.value: Xaymaca, land of wood and water, home of the Taino

quickFacts.label: World Heritage Site
quickFacts.value: Blue and John Crow Mountains, inscribed 2015

eras.title: Xaymaca: The Taino Island
eras.standfirst: Long before Europe, the Arawak-speaking Taino settled the island they called Xaymaca, building villages, gardens, and a society of caciques that Columbus's arrival would destroy within a generation.
body: Centuries before European contact, Arawak-speaking peoples migrated from South America through the Caribbean and settled the island, which they called Xaymaca, often rendered as the land of wood and water. The people now known as the Taino lived in villages led by hereditary chiefs called caciques, sustaining themselves by fishing and by cultivating corn, cassava, and other crops. By the close of the fifteenth century their settlements were spread across the island, and population estimates run into the tens of thousands.

Taino society possessed its own religion, art, and political order. They worshipped ancestral and nature spirits known as zemis, played a ritual ball game, and produced carved wooden and stone objects, pottery, and ornaments. Their language has left an enduring mark on the wider world, contributing words such as hammock, hurricane, barbecue, and canoe. The rugged interior, including the highlands that would later shelter the Maroons, formed part of their world.

This world was shattered by the arrival of the Spanish. On 5 May 1494, during his second voyage, Christopher Columbus reached the island and named its north-coast harbour Santa Gloria, near present-day St Ann's Bay. Within decades the Taino population collapsed under the combined weight of enslavement, forced labour, warfare, and Old World diseases such as smallpox, against which they had no immunity. By the mid-sixteenth century the indigenous society of Xaymaca had been all but extinguished.

events.title: Taino settlement of Xaymaca
events.summary: Arawak-speaking peoples settle the island they call Xaymaca, building villages led by caciques and farming cassava and corn.

events.title: Columbus reaches Jamaica
events.summary: On his second voyage Christopher Columbus arrives off the north coast, claiming the island for Spain and naming a harbour Santa Gloria.

pullquote.text: Xaymaca, the land of wood and water.
pullquote.attribution: Taino name for the island, as recorded by early chroniclers

eras.title: Spanish Colony and English Conquest
eras.standfirst: A neglected Spanish possession for a century and a half, the island was seized by Cromwell's England in 1655 and turned into a sugar colony whose pirate capital, Port Royal, became the wickedest city on earth.
body: Granted to the Columbus family, the island was for Spain a quiet backwater, valued mainly as a supply base for food and hides on the route to richer colonies. The first permanent European settlement, Sevilla la Nueva, was founded on the north coast in 1509, and the capital later shifted inland to Santiago de la Vega, today's Spanish Town. As the Taino vanished, the Spanish began importing enslaved Africans, planting the seeds of the slave society that would define the island's future.

In 1655, as part of Oliver Cromwell's Western Design against Spanish power in the Caribbean, an English expedition under Admiral William Penn and General Robert Venables captured the island. Spanish resistance lingered for some years, and many of the Spaniards' formerly enslaved people fled to the mountains, forming the first Maroon communities. Under English rule the island was rapidly transformed into a plantation colony, its economy built on sugar, on the forced labour of enslaved Africans, and on the Atlantic slave trade.

On the sand spit at the mouth of Kingston Harbour, the port of Port Royal grew rich as a haven for privateers and pirates, among them the Welsh buccaneer Henry Morgan, who preyed on Spanish shipping with the colony's blessing. Its taverns, brothels, and merchant houses earned it a reputation as the storehouse of the West Indies and, notoriously, the wickedest city on earth. That reputation ended abruptly on 7 June 1692, when a violent earthquake and tsunami plunged much of the town beneath the sea, killing thousands.

events.title: Sevilla la Nueva founded
events.summary: Spain establishes its first permanent settlement on the north coast, beginning a colonial era that all but exterminates the Taino.

events.title: English conquest
events.summary: An English force under Penn and Venables seizes the island for Cromwell, launching the sugar-and-slavery plantation economy.

events.title: Port Royal earthquake
events.summary: A powerful earthquake and tsunami sink much of the buccaneer city of Port Royal, killing thousands and ending its golden age.

pullquote.text: The storehouse and treasury of the West Indies, and one of the wickedest places on earth.

eras.title: Sugar, Slavery, and Resistance
eras.standfirst: As Jamaica became Britain's richest sugar colony, enslaved Africans resisted ceaselessly: the Maroons won their mountain freedom by treaty, while Tacky and Sam Sharpe led revolts that helped break slavery itself.
body: Through the eighteenth century the island became the most valuable sugar colony in the British Empire, its plantations worked by hundreds of thousands of enslaved Africans whose labour produced the white gold of sugar alongside coffee, pimento, and rum. The enslaved population came to vastly outnumber the white planter class, and the colony's wealth was matched only by the brutality of the system that created it. Resistance was constant, from sabotage and flight to open rebellion.

In the rugged interior, the Maroons, communities of self-liberated people descended from those who had fled Spanish and English plantations, waged a guerrilla war from the Blue and John Crow Mountains. Led in the east by the revered Windward leader Nanny and in the west by Cudjoe, they fought the colonial militia to a standstill in the First Maroon War. Treaties signed in 1739 and 1740 granted the Maroons land and autonomy, recognising a Black free society within a slave colony, though at the bitter price of agreeing to return future runaways.

Major uprisings punctuated the era. In 1760 Tacky's Revolt, led by an Akan man of that name, convulsed the island and was suppressed only with Maroon help. The greatest came at Christmas 1831, when the Baptist deacon Samuel Sharpe led a general strike and rebellion of perhaps sixty thousand enslaved people, the Baptist War. Its violent suppression and Sharpe's execution helped move British opinion decisively against slavery. The Abolition Act of 1833 ended slavery across the empire, and after a transitional period of apprenticeship, full freedom came on 1 August 1838.

events.title: Maroon treaties
events.summary: Treaties with Cudjoe's Leeward Maroons and Nanny's Windward Maroons end the First Maroon War, granting them land and autonomy.

events.title: Tacky's Revolt
events.summary: A major slave rebellion led by the Akan leader Tacky sweeps the island before being crushed with the aid of the Maroons.

events.title: The Baptist War
events.summary: Samuel Sharpe leads a Christmas rebellion of tens of thousands; its suppression hastens the end of slavery in the empire.

events.title: Emancipation
events.summary: After the Abolition Act of 1833 and a period of apprenticeship, the enslaved people of Jamaica gain full freedom.

figures.name: Nanny of the Maroons
figures.blurb: Akan-born military and spiritual leader who guided the Windward Maroons through their fiercest resistance to the British from the Blue Mountains. Jamaica's only female National Hero, she gives her name to the Nanny Town heritage of the World Heritage site.

figures.name: Samuel Sharpe
figures.blurb: Self-educated enslaved Baptist deacon who led the Christmas Rebellion, or Baptist War, of 1831-32. Executed at Montego Bay in 1832, he is honoured as a National Hero whose revolt helped force the abolition of slavery.

pullquote.text: I would rather die upon yonder gallows than live in slavery.
pullquote.attribution: Words attributed to Samuel Sharpe before his execution, 1832

eras.title: Crown Colony, Morant Bay, and Garvey
eras.standfirst: Freedom did not bring equality. The Morant Bay rebellion of 1865 ushered in direct crown rule, while Marcus Garvey forged a global Pan-African movement and labour unrest set the stage for self-government.
body: Emancipation left the formerly enslaved majority poor and politically powerless, struggling for land and justice against a planter elite and a colonial administration. Discontent erupted in October 1865 at Morant Bay, where Paul Bogle, a Native Baptist deacon, led a protest that became an armed uprising. Governor Edward Eyre's reprisals were savage: hundreds were killed or executed, Bogle was hanged, and the mixed-race assemblyman George William Gordon was court-martialled and executed on dubious charges. In the aftermath the old assembly was abolished and Jamaica became a directly ruled crown colony.

From this society of inequality rose Marcus Mosiah Garvey, who founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association in 1914 to promote Black pride, economic self-reliance, and unity across the African diaspora. Building a movement of millions on both sides of the Atlantic, Garvey became one of the most influential Pan-Africanists in history. His message of African redemption helped inspire the Rastafari movement, which emerged in Jamaica in the 1930s after the coronation of Ethiopia's Emperor Haile Selassie I, born Ras Tafari.

By the 1930s economic hardship deepened by the Great Depression fed mounting unrest. In 1938 island-wide labour rebellions among dock and plantation workers shook the colony and gave birth to modern Jamaican politics. Out of this crucible came two towering and rival cousins: Alexander Bustamante, who organised workers into a powerful trade union, and Norman Manley, who founded the People's National Party. Their movements would become the Jamaica Labour Party and the PNP, the twin engines of the road to self-rule.

events.title: Morant Bay rebellion
events.summary: Paul Bogle's uprising is brutally crushed; Bogle and George William Gordon are executed, and Jamaica becomes a crown colony.

events.title: Garvey founds the UNIA
events.summary: Marcus Garvey establishes the Universal Negro Improvement Association, building the largest Pan-African movement of its age.

events.title: Birth of Rastafari
events.summary: The Rastafari movement emerges in Jamaica after the crowning of Haile Selassie I, drawing on Garvey's vision of African redemption.

events.title: Labour rebellions
events.summary: Island-wide worker uprisings give rise to Bustamante's union and Manley's PNP, founding modern Jamaican party politics.

figures.name: Marcus Garvey
figures.blurb: Founder of the Universal Negro Improvement Association and the most influential Black nationalist of his era, preaching pride, self-reliance, and the unity of the African diaspora. He became Jamaica's first National Hero.

figures.name: Paul Bogle
figures.blurb: Native Baptist deacon who led the Morant Bay rebellion of 1865 against post-emancipation injustice. Executed in its aftermath, he is honoured as a National Hero of Jamaica.

pullquote.text: Up, you mighty race, accomplish what you will.

eras.title: Self-Government and Independence
eras.standfirst: Universal suffrage, a brief federation, and a decisive referendum carried Jamaica from colony to nation, achieving full independence within the Commonwealth on 6 August 1962.
body: The labour upheavals of 1938 forced constitutional change. In 1944 Jamaica gained a new constitution and universal adult suffrage, the first British Caribbean colony to do so, and held its first elections under the franchise. Through the 1940s and 1950s the rival Jamaica Labour Party and People's National Party alternated in office, building the institutions of self-government as the colony moved steadily toward internal autonomy and a growing national consciousness.

Britain hoped its Caribbean colonies would gain independence together through a single federation. The West Indies Federation, with Jamaica as its largest member, was inaugurated in 1958. But many Jamaicans feared their interests would be subordinated to smaller islands, and on 19 September 1961 the electorate voted in a referendum to withdraw. Jamaica's departure doomed the federation, which dissolved in 1962, and cleared the path to separate nationhood.

A general election in April 1962 returned Alexander Bustamante's Jamaica Labour Party to power, and at the stroke of midnight on 6 August 1962 the Union Jack was lowered and the new black, green, and gold flag of Jamaica raised. The island became an independent realm within the Commonwealth, retaining the British monarch as head of state through a governor-general, with Bustamante as its first prime minister. After more than three centuries, colonial rule was over.

events.title: Universal adult suffrage
events.summary: A new constitution grants every adult the vote, the first in the British Caribbean, and Jamaica holds its first elections under it.

events.title: West Indies Federation
events.summary: Jamaica joins the federation of British Caribbean colonies as its largest member, an experiment in regional union.

events.title: Referendum to leave the federation
events.summary: Jamaicans vote to withdraw from the West Indies Federation, dooming it and opening the way to separate independence.

events.title: Independence
events.summary: Jamaica becomes an independent nation within the Commonwealth, with Alexander Bustamante as its first prime minister.

figures.name: Alexander Bustamante
figures.role: Trade unionist and first Prime Minister
figures.blurb: Charismatic labour leader who built a powerful trade union and the Jamaica Labour Party from the unrest of 1938. He became the first prime minister of independent Jamaica in 1962 and a National Hero.

figures.name: Norman Manley
figures.role: Statesman and founder of the PNP
figures.blurb: Lawyer and nationalist who founded the People's National Party in 1938 and, as premier, led the constitutional drive toward independence. Cousin and rival of Bustamante, he is a National Hero of Jamaica.

pullquote.attribution: National motto of Jamaica, adopted at independence

eras.title: Independent Jamaica and Global Culture
eras.standfirst: Since independence Jamaica has navigated political rivalry and economic strain while projecting extraordinary global influence through reggae, Bob Marley, the Rastafari faith, and the fastest sprinters on earth.
body: Independent Jamaica became a competitive two-party democracy in which the Jamaica Labour Party and the People's National Party alternated in power, often amid sharp ideological and social conflict. The 1970s brought the democratic-socialist programme of PNP leader Michael Manley, the son of Norman Manley, along with economic difficulty, political violence, and emigration. Successive governments have grappled with debt, unemployment, and crime, even as Jamaica built durable democratic institutions and a strong national identity.

From this small island flowed a cultural wave of global proportions. Out of ska and rocksteady emerged reggae, the music that carried the spiritual and political message of the Rastafari movement to the world. Its supreme ambassador was Bob Marley, whose recordings with the Wailers, released internationally from the early 1970s, made reggae a worldwide language of resistance and unity. His compilation Legend remains the best-selling reggae album in history, and reggae itself was recognised by UNESCO as part of humanity's intangible cultural heritage.

Jamaica's global reach extends to the running track, where a nation of fewer than three million people has produced an unbroken line of champion sprinters. The phenomenon reached its apex in Usain Bolt, who from 2008 won the Olympic 100-metre and 200-metre titles at three consecutive Games and set world records that still stand, becoming the fastest man ever timed. In the natural realm, the Blue and John Crow Mountains, refuge of the Maroons, were inscribed in 2015 as Jamaica's first UNESCO World Heritage Site, binding the island's deep history to its present.

events.title: Two-party democracy
events.summary: Independent Jamaica settles into a competitive democracy contested by the JLP and the PNP, with peaceful alternations of power.

events.title: Reggae goes global
events.summary: Bob Marley and the Wailers bring reggae and the Rastafari message to a worldwide audience, making Jamaica a cultural superpower.

events.title: Bolt's sprinting dynasty
events.summary: Usain Bolt wins the 100m and 200m at three straight Olympics, cementing Jamaica's dominance of world sprinting.

events.title: World Heritage inscription
events.summary: The Blue and John Crow Mountains, refuge of the Maroons, become Jamaica's first UNESCO World Heritage Site.

figures.name: Bob Marley
figures.blurb: Singer, songwriter, and Rastafari adherent whose music with the Wailers made reggae a global force and Jamaica's most powerful cultural export. His Legend remains the best-selling reggae album of all time.

figures.name: Usain Bolt
figures.blurb: Trelawny-born sprinter widely regarded as the greatest of all time, winner of Olympic gold in the 100m and 200m at three consecutive Games and holder of world records that still stand.

pullquote.text: One good thing about music, when it hits you, you feel no pain.

figures.name: Nanny of the Maroons
figures.blurb: Akan-born military and spiritual leader of the Windward Maroons who fought the British to a treaty securing Maroon freedom in the eastern mountains. Jamaica's only female National Hero.

figures.name: Marcus Garvey
figures.blurb: Founder of the Universal Negro Improvement Association and the most influential Black nationalist of the twentieth century, whose vision of African redemption shaped Jamaica and the wider diaspora.

figures.name: Bob Marley
figures.blurb: The supreme ambassador of reggae, whose music carried the Rastafari message worldwide and made Jamaica a cultural power far beyond its size.

sources.label: The Federal Archives Fonds, 1958-1962 (West Indies Federation)
